title: Florentines
subtitle: Caramel and nut wafers with a dark chocolate backing
excerpt: Florentines are made by cooking a caramel base with cream and sugar, mixing in nuts and dried fruit, then spooning into rounds on a baking sheet. The chocolate backing is applied after baking and cooling, and scored with a fork in the traditional wave pattern before it sets.
sourceNotes: Florentines are an Italian or French confection — the precise origin is disputed, but they appear widely in European domestic cookery from the nineteenth century and became a British baking staple through the twentieth century. They are documented in British domestic cookbooks from the 1950s onward as a Christmas and gift biscuit.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keep in an airtight tin, layered with baking paper, for up to 2 weeks.

glossaryTerms.definition: The traditional finish on the chocolate backing of a florentine: a fork is drawn through the melted chocolate in a continuous S-wave pattern as it starts to set. The scoring creates the ridged texture associated with florentines and also increases the surface area so the chocolate sets with a slight sheen.

content.text: The chocolate backing on a florentine is applied when the biscuit is cold, then marked with

as it begins to set. Spread the chocolate while it is still fully liquid, then wait until the chocolate is tacky but not yet dry before drawing the fork through it. If you score too early the pattern fills in; too late and it cracks.
Make the caramel base and bake

Heat the oven to 180°C (160°C fan). Line two baking trays with baking paper. Melt {{unsalted-butter}}, {{caster-sugar}}, and {{double-cream}} in a small saucepan over medium heat, stirring until the sugar dissolves. Bring to a boil and cook for 1 minute until slightly thickened. Remove from heat and stir in {{almonds-flaked}}, {{glace-cherries}}, {{mixed-peel}}, and {{plain-flour}}. Drop level teaspoons onto the trays, leaving 5 cm between each — they spread significantly. Bake for 10–12 minutes until spread flat and deep gold at the edges. Leave to cool on the tray for 5 minutes, then transfer carefully to a wire rack. Cool completely.

Melt {{dark-chocolate}} over a pan of barely simmering water or in a microwave in 30-second bursts. Turn the florentines flat-side up. Spread a thin layer of chocolate over each one. As the chocolate begins to set, draw a fork through it in S-curves. Leave flat to set completely — about 1 hour at room temperature, or 20 minutes in the fridge.
